narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your undergrad years and prepping for the LSAT—it's tough to hear that your application to law school didn't make the cut because our predictive model saw a 69.9% chance of struggling on the bar exam down the line. Picture it like this: the model looked at patterns from thousands of students just like you and painted a story of your journey based on a few key chapters from your profile. The biggest red flag was your Year 3 decile of 5.0, which stood out as the strongest pull toward potential failure, suggesting that even as you progressed, your grades settled in the middle of the pack compared to peers. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile of 4.0, reinforcing a sense that academic momentum started modestly and didn't surge ahead. Your upper-middle family income added another layer, as the model associates that background with certain challenges in pushing through the rigors of law school. Your LSAT score of 35.0, while respectable, nudged the odds further toward difficulty, as did your white racial background and undergraduate GPA of 3.3, each contributing their weight to the picture of a tougher path to the bar. Being full-time in your studies pulled in the same direction, hinting at the intensity without the buffer of part-time flexibility. The one bright spot was your gender as male, which the model saw as a gentle push toward success, but it wasn't quite enough to tip the scales against the heavier influences. Together, these pieces formed a narrative of steady but not standout performance, one where the model worried the bar's high stakes might prove overwhelming. This isn't a judgment on your potential or heart—many have rewritten their stories from here—but it explains why the school had to make this call to protect their bar passage rates. If you share more about steps you're taking to boost those deciles or LSAT prep, there might be ways to shift the odds for next time. Hang in there; your story's just getting started.
